steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage establishes that native fruit-eating birds are gone, that plant survival depends on birds dispersing seeds, and that non-native fruit-eating birds are now common on Oahu. The blank must follow logically from these facts — the correct answer is the one the studies' findings directly support.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'are dropping higher numbers of native forest plant seeds around the island than native bird species did in the past': The passage gives no data comparing seed-drop quantities between native and non-native birds. This is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice B — 'show significantly more interest in eating the fruits of native forest plants than in eating the fruits of non-native ones': The passage never mentions the birds' food preferences or any comparison to non-native plants. Unsupported. ✗
  • Choice C — 'may be necessary for the continued survival of vulnerable forest plant species, such as the olona shrub': Native birds are extinct, plants need birds for seed dispersal to survive, and non-native birds are now filling that role — so those non-native birds may be what keeps species like olona alive. This follows directly from the passage. ✓
  • Choice D — 'may also engage in other activities that affect the ability of olona shrubs and other vulnerable forest plants to continue to spread to new areas': "Other activities" introduces speculation about unmentioned behaviors. The passage discusses only seed dispersal. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
